perturbation.original_text: I agree that Motorola, and its officers, agents, and employees are not liable for injuries or property damage resulting from my attendance at the event. I agree to release and hold all of the above named persons and entities harmless from any claim for injury, damage or loss of any kind.
perturbation.changed_text: I will NOT disclose, share, publish or utilize any Confidential Motorola Information without the prior authorization from Motorola Mobility. I agree that Motorola, and its officers, agents, and employees are not liable for injuries or property damage resulting from my attendance at the event. I agree to release and hold all of the above named persons and entities harmless from any claim for injury, damage or loss of any kind.
perturbation.explanation: The liability waiver is moved to the section outlining the agreement not to disclose confidential information. This is an unusual and potentially misleading location for a liability waiver, making it less likely to be noticed and understood by the visitor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding conspicuousness and enforceability of waivers.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 211 and general contract law principles state that standardized agreements are enforceable unless the party has reason to believe that the other party would not assent to such terms if they knew of them, especially if the terms are hidden. Placing a liability waiver in a section about confidentiality makes it less conspicuous and harder for the visitor to understand its implications, potentially rendering it less enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves a liability waiver into a section on confidential information, potentially obscuring its significance. This creates a structural flaw, as the waiver's placement makes it less likely to be noticed and understood. The Law Explanation highlights Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 211 and general contract law, emphasizing that standardized agreements are enforceable unless a party has reason to believe the other would not assent to the terms, especially if hidden. The moved waiver could be considered a hidden term. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions mutual assent as a basic element of an enforceable contract, suggesting that if a party doesn't fully understand a term (like the liability waiver due to its placement), mutual assent could be questioned.

perturbation.original_text: I will NOT take photographs or recordings.
perturbation.changed_text: I will NOT go into restricted areas without prior and proper authorization. (this includes labs and CXD areas). I will NOT take photographs or recordings.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the prohibition of taking photographs to the section regarding restricted areas. This makes it seem that the only place where photos/recordings are not allowed is in restricted areas, which may not be the intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract interpretation - ambiguity construed against the drafter
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 206 states that in choosing among the reasonable meanings of a promise or agreement or a term thereof, that meaning is generally preferred which operates against the party who supplies the words or from whom a writing otherwise proceeds. Placing it near restrictions may cause a reasonable person to infer that photos can be taken anywhere but restricted areas.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, by relocating the photography prohibition to the section about restricted areas, creates a structural flaw that implies photography and recordings are permitted outside of these areas. This contradicts the original intent, which was a blanket ban. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 206 favors interpretations against the party who supplied the words, suggesting a reasonable person might infer that photos are allowed elsewhere.

perturbation.original_text: I will NOT bring in a minor without prior authorization.I will stay with my Motorola approved host at all times.
perturbation.changed_text: I will be respectful of others who are working. I will NOT bring in a minor without prior authorization.I will stay with my Motorola approved host at all times.
perturbation.explanation: The rule about minors is moved into the section about being respectful. This is an odd pairing of rules and it may make the section about minors less noticeable. Moving it to the respect section makes the importance of the minor section less important and makes it look like an extension of the respect.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract interpretation - the 'ejusdem generis' rule.
perturbation.law_citation: Black's Law Dictionary definition of 'ejusdem generis'
perturbation.law_explanation: The ejusdem generis rule states that when general words follow an enumeration of specific items, the general words are construed to embrace only objects similar in nature to those objects enumerated. By placing the statement in the respect section, one can assume the minor clause is an extension of that clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ejusdem generis | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ejusdem generis Ejusdem generis (ee-joose-dem gen-ris) is a Latin phrase that means “of the same kind.” The statutory and constitutional construction principle of “ejusdem generis” states that where general words or phrases follow a number of specific words or phrases, the general words are specifically construed as limited and apply only to persons or things of the same kind or class as those expressly mentioned. For example, if a law refers to automobiles, trucks, tractors, motorcycles, and other motor-powered vehicles, a court might use ejusdem generis to hold that such vehicles would not include airplanes, because the list included only land-based transportation. Ejusdem generis is an interpretive guide for a contract under New York law. The rule is used only to help determine whether there is intent ; if intent is found, ejusdem generis does not subvert intent. [Last reviewed in February of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords statutory interpretation Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS wex definitions legal theory Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the rule about minors into a section about being respectful, potentially weakening its legal force. The Law Explanation suggests this could invoke the 'ejusdem generis' rule, implying the minor clause is merely an extension of the respect clause. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'ejusdem generis' as a principle where general words following specific ones are limited to the same kind. By embedding the rule about minors (a specific legal obligation) within a general clause about respect, its importance and enforceability are diminished, creating a structural flaw that contradicts the original intent. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the relocation obscures a specific legal obligation within a more general, less legally forceful context, potentially making the contract non-compliant due to unclear placement of a legal requirement.
